blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (١)
The Besieger! (1)
مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٢)
What is the Besieger?! (2)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٣)
What will show you what is the Besieger?! (3)
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعَادٌۢ بِٱلْقَارِعَةِ (٤)
Thamudand ‘Ad denied the Cataclysm. (4)
فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِٱلطَّاغِيَةِ (٥)
As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the Cry. (5)
وَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ (٦)
And as for ‘Ad, they were destroyed by a fierce icy gale, (6)
سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا فَتَرَى ٱلْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ (٧)
which He clamped on them for seven gruelling nights and eight days, so that you could see the people there lying about prostrate as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees. (7)
فَهَلْ تَرَىٰ لَهُم مِّنۢ بَاقِيَةٍ (٨)
So do you see any remaining trace of them? (8)

Content of Surah al-Haqqah

The discussions in this Surah revolve around three axes:

The "First Axis," which is the most important theme of this Surah, concerns matters related to the Resurrection and many of its characteristics. Therefore, three names of the Day of Judgment—namely "al-Haqqah," "al-Qari'ah," and "al-Waqi'ah"—appear in this Surah.

The "Second Axis" consists of discussions regarding the fate of past disbelieving nations, especially the people of 'Ad, Thamud, and Pharaoh, which include powerful and emphasized warnings for all disbelievers and deniers of the Resurrection.

The "Third Axis" involves discussions about the greatness of the Quran, the status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the punishment of those who deny him.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

من قرأ سورة الحاقة حاسبه الله حسابا يسيرا

"Whoever recites Surah al-Haqqah, Allah will make his accounting easy on the Day of Judgment." [1] And in another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated:

اكثروا من قراءة الحاقة، فان قراءتها فى الفرائض و النوافل من الايمان بالله و رسوله، و لم يسلب قارئها دينه حتى يلقى الله‌

"Recite Surah al-Haqqah frequently, for its recitation in obligatory and supererogatory prayers is a sign of faith in Allah and His Messenger, and the one who recites it will not have his religion stripped away until he meets Allah." [2]

[1] and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 342.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

الْحَاقَّةُ (1) مَا الْحَاقَّةُ (2) وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ (3) كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَ عادٌ بِالْقارِعَةِ (4)

فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ (5) وَ أَمَّا عادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عاتِيَةٍ (6) سَخَّرَها عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيالٍ وَ ثَمانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُوماً فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيها صَرْعى‌ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَةٍ (7) فَهَلْ تَرى‌ لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَةٍ (8)

Commentary

A Transgressing Punishment for a Rebellious People!

This Surah begins with the issue of the Resurrection, using a new title, saying:

"The Inevitable Reality" (الْحَاقَّةُ).

"What is the Inevitable Reality?" (مَا الْحَاقَّةُ) [1]

[1]: Regarding the syntax of this sentence, several possibilities have been suggested, the most appropriate of which is that "الْحَاقَّةُ" is the subject (mubtada), "ما" is the interrogative second subject, and the "الحاقة" following it is the predicate of the second subject, and the entire phrase serves as the predicate for the first subject.

"And what can make you know what the Inevitable Reality is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ). Almost all commentators have interpreted "Haqqah" to mean the Day of Resurrection, because it is a day that will certainly occur, similar to the expression "al-Waqi'ah" in Surah al-Waqi'ah. This same expression also appears in verse 16 of this very Surah, and all of these indicate the certainty of that Great Day.

The expression "مَا الْحَاقَّةُ" is used to demonstrate the magnitude of that day, just as in everyday speech we might say: "So-and-so is a man; and what a man!" meaning there are no bounds to describing his manhood.

The expression "ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ" is again for further emphasis on the greatness of the events of that momentous day, to the extent that even the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is addressed: "You do not know what that day is like." And the truth is exactly this: perceiving the events of the Resurrection is impossible for us, the prisoners of the prison of this world, just as perceiving matters related to the world is impossible for a fetus in the mother's womb through any description. [1] Another possibility in the interpretation of these verses—though few commentators have accepted it—is that "الْحَاقَّةُ" refers to the punishments that suddenly

[1]: Some commentators have said that the phrase "ما أَدْراكَ" is used throughout the Quran in places where the matter is known and certain, whereas "وَ ما يُدْرِيكَ" appears in cases where it is unknown (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 343). Other commentators, such as al-Qurtubi, have also narrated this meaning.

overtake rebellious criminals and arrogant tyrants in this world, just as "al-Qari'ah" in the following verse has been interpreted by some commentators with this same meaning. Incidentally, this interpretation is more consistent with the upcoming verses that speak of the crushing punishments of the people of 'Ad, Thamud, Pharaoh, and the people of Lot.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we also read: "الْحَاقَّةُ: the warning of the descent of punishment," similar to what was said regarding the people of Pharaoh (Surah Ghafir, verse 45), where He says: وَ حاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذابِ:

"And the evil punishment encompassed the people of Pharaoh." [1]

Then, it proceeds to describe the fate of the nations who denied the Day of Resurrection (or the descent of divine punishment in this world), adding: "Thamud and 'Ad denied the Striking Calamity" (كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَ عادٌ بِالْقارِعَةِ).

"As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the overpowering blast" (فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ). The "People of Thamud" were a nation living in a mountainous region between Hijaz and Sham. Prophet Salih عليه السلام was sent to them, but they never believed and rose up in opposition to him, even demanding: "If you are truthful, bring down the punishment you promise us!" At that moment, a "devastating thunderbolt" overcame them, and in a few moments, it cast tremors upon their palaces and fortified

[1]: Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, Vol. 2, p. 383 (Note that "Haqqah" and "Haq" are from the same root).

homes, crushing them all, and their lifeless bodies fell to the ground.

It is noteworthy that the Quran considers the factor of the destruction of this rebellious nation to be a "rebellious punishment" (al-Taghiyah). This rebellious punishment is mentioned in Surah al-A'raf, verse 78, as "Rajfah" (earthquake), in verse 13 of Fussilat as "Sa'iqah" (thunderbolt), and in verse 67 of Hud as "Sayhah" (shout), all of which essentially refer to the same thing, as a thunderbolt is always accompanied by a great sound and causes tremors at the point where it strikes, and it is a transgressing punishment. [1]

Then it moves to the fate of the "People of 'Ad," a nation that lived in the land of "Ahqaf" (in the Arabian Peninsula or Yemen). They had tall statures, strong bodies, prosperous cities, lush and green lands, and vibrant gardens. Their prophet was Prophet Hud عليه السلام. They also reached such a level of rebellion and defiance that Allah folded the scroll of their lives with a painful punishment, the description of which appears in these verses. First, He says: "And as for 'Ad, they were destroyed by a screaming, violent, cold, and poisonous wind" (وَ أَمَّا عادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عاتِيَةٍ). "Sarsar" refers to winds that are cold, loud, or poisonous, and commentators have mentioned all three meanings in its interpretation; a combination of them is also possible.

"Atiyah" is from the root "Utu," meaning rebellious—not rebellious against Allah's command, but rebellious in comparison to the standards of ordinary breezes and winds.

[1]: The story of the "People of Thamud" is detailed in Vol. 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pp. 234–241.

Then, providing another description of this crushing gale, He adds: "Allah imposed it upon them for seven nights and eight days in succession and as a root-cutter" (سَخَّرَها عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيالٍ وَ ثَمانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُوماً).

"Husuman" is from the root "Husm," meaning to eliminate the traces of something. If a sword is called "Husam," it is for this reason. Sometimes, cauterizing a wound to burn away its root is also called "Husm." Here, it means that these seven nights and eight days consecutively crushed, shattered, and uprooted the extensive and prosperous life of this great nation. [1] The result was as the Quran says: "So you would see the people therein fallen, as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees!" (فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيها صَرْعى‌ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَةٍ).

What a fascinating comparison, which specifies both their great stature and their being uprooted, as well as their being hollow in the face of divine punishments, such that the gale moved them easily.

"Khawiyah" is from the root "Khawa'," originally meaning to become empty. This expression is also used for hungry stomachs, stars devoid of rain (according to the belief of the pre-Islamic Arabs), and a hollow walnut.

[1]: Note that "Husuman" is an adjective for "seven nights and eight days," though some have considered it a "circumstantial state" (hal) for "wind" or a "reasoning object" (maf'ul lahu).

And in the final verse, He adds: "Do you see of them any remains?" (فَهَلْ تَرى‌ لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَةٍ) [1] Indeed, today not only is there no trace of the people of 'Ad, but nothing remains of the ruins of their prosperous cities, magnificent buildings, and green fields.

Regarding the story of the people of 'Ad, we have discussed it in detail in Vol. 9, pp. 140–148 (under verses 58–60 of Surah Hud), and also in Vol. 20, pp. 233–241.

[1]: "Baqiyah" is an adjective for an implied noun, originally "nafs baqiyah" (a surviving soul).

Ayahs

وَجَآءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَن قَبْلَهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتُ بِٱلْخَاطِئَةِ (٩)
Then Pharaoh and those who were before him, and the towns that were overturned, brought about iniquity. (9)
فَعَصَوْا۟ رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رَّابِيَةً (١٠)
They disobeyed the apostle of their Lord, so He seized them with a terrible seizing. (10)
إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَا ٱلْمَآءُ حَمَلْنَـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْجَارِيَةِ (١١)
Indeed when the Flood rose high, We carried you in a floating ark, (11)
لِنَجْعَلَهَا لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَهَآ أُذُنٌ وَٰعِيَةٌ (١٢)
that We might make it a reminder for you, and that receptive ears might remember it. (12)

Commentary

Where are the Hearing Ears?

After mentioning a portion of the history of the peoples of ‘Ad and Thamud, the discourse turns to other nations such as the people of "Noah" and the people of "Lot," in order to provide another lesson for those with wakeful hearts. It says: "And Pharaoh and those before him and the overturned cities (the people of Lot) committed great sins."

(وَ جاءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ مَنْ قَبْلَهُ وَ الْمُؤْتَفِكاتُ بِالْخاطِئَةِ)

"Khāti’ah" (خاطئة) means error or sin (both carry an infinitive meaning), and what is meant by error here is polytheism, disbelief, injustice, corruption, and various types of sins.

"Mu’tafikāt" (مؤتفكات) is the plural of "Mu’tafikah," derived from the root "I’tifāk" (ائتفاك), meaning revolution or being overturned. Here, it refers to the cities of the people of Lot, which were overturned by a severe earthquake.

The phrase "those before him" (مَنْ قَبْلَهُ) refers to the nations that existed before Pharaoh, such as the people of "Shu’ayb" and tyrants like Nimrod.

Then it adds: "And they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord, so He seized them with a swelling seizure" (فَعَصَوْا رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رابِيَةً). The Pharaohs opposed "Moses" and "Aaron," the inhabitants of the cities of "Sodom" opposed Prophet "Lot," and other nations likewise defied the commands of their respective prophets. Each group of these rebels was afflicted with a specific type of punishment: the Pharaohs were drowned in the waves of the "Nile"—which was the source of life, prosperity, and blessing for their country—and the people of Lot were annihilated by a severe "earthquake" followed by a "rain of stones."

"Rābiyah" (رابية) and "Ribā" (ربا) come from the same root, meaning increase or addition. Here, it refers to a punishment that was exceedingly harsh and severe.

The account of Pharaoh’s people is mentioned in many Surahs of the Quran, most extensively in verses 10 to 68 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 15, p. 195 onwards).

Also in Surah Al-A'raf, verses 103 to 137 (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, p. 277 onwards).

And in Surah Ta-Ha, verses 24 to 79 (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 185 onwards).

The story of the people of Lot is also found in many Surahs, including verses 61 to 77 of Surah Al-Hijr (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 11, p. 106 onwards).

And in Surah Hud, verses 77 to 83 (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 9, p. 178).

Finally, it makes a brief reference to the fate of the people of Noah and their painful punishment, saying: "Indeed, when the water overflowed, We carried you in the sailing ship" (إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَى الْماءُ حَمَلْناكُمْ فِي الْجارِيَةِ).

The overflow of water was such that dark clouds covered the sky, and rain descended as if floods were pouring from the heavens; springs also gushed forth from the earth. These two waters joined together, and everything was submerged—the gardens, fields, palaces, and houses of the rebellious people. The only group that was saved were the believers who boarded the ship with Noah.

The expression "We carried you" (حَمَلْناكُمْ) is an allusion to our ancestors and forefathers, for if they had not been saved, we would not exist today. [^1]

Then, pointing to the primary purpose of these punishments, it adds: "That We might make it for you a reminder" (لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً).

"And that a conscious ear might retain it" (وَ تَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ).

He never intended to merely take revenge upon them; rather, the goal was the education of human beings, guiding them on the path of perfection, showing the way, and bringing them to the desired destination.

"Ta’iyahā" (تعيها) is from the root "Wa’y" (وعي). As "Raghib" states in Al-Mufradat and "Ibn Manzur" in Lisan al-Arab, it originally means to preserve something in the heart. Subsequently, every vessel is called "Wi’ā’" (وعاء) because it holds something within itself. In the verse under discussion, this quality is attributed to "ears"—ears that hear truths and retain them within.

In other words, sometimes a person hears a word and immediately casts it out of the ear, as we say in common parlance: "It went in one ear and out the other." But sometimes, one reflects upon it, places it in the heart, and considers it a lamp for the path of life. This is what is expressed by the term "Wa’y."

Key Points

1- Another Virtue of Ali (عليه السلام)

It is recorded in many famous Islamic books, both of Tafsir and Hadith, that when the above verse (وَ تَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ) was revealed, the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

"I asked my Lord to make it the ear of Ali."

Following this, Ali (عليه السلام) used to say:

"I never heard anything from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) after that which I forgot; rather, I always remembered it." [^1] In Ghayat al-Maram, sixteen hadiths are narrated in this regard from both Shi'a and Sunni sources. "Muhaddith Bahrani" in Tafsir al-Burhan quotes from "Muhammad ibn Abbas" that thirty hadiths have been narrated on this subject from both the general (Sunni) and specific (Shi'a) chains of transmission.

This is a great virtue for the great leader of Islam, Ali (عليه السلام), who was the treasure-chest of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) secrets and the heir to all the knowledge of the Messenger of God (صلى الله عليه وآله). For this reason, after the Prophet, whenever the Islamic community faced scientific or intellectual difficulties, both supporters and opponents would seek refuge in him and ask him to solve the problem, as is detailed in history books.

2- The Proportion Between "Sin" and "Punishment"

The expressions used in the above verses are noteworthy: regarding the punishment of the people of Thamud, "Tāghiyah" (the overwhelming); for the people of ‘Ad, "‘Ātiyah" (the furious); for the people of Pharaoh and Lot, "Rābiyah" (the swelling); and for the people of Noah, the expression "the water overflowed" (طَغَى الْماءُ). All of these contain the concept of rebellion and transgression. Thus, the punishment of these rebellious nations is considered the "rebellion" of certain blessings of life, whether water, wind, earth, or fire.

These expressions emphasize the reality that the punishments of this world and the hereafter are an embodiment of our own actions, and it is the deeds of humans that return to them.

[^1]: Some have therefore said there is an omitted word in the verse, and the implied meaning is "We carried your forefathers."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6743; Majma' al-Bayan; Ruh al-Bayan; Ruh al-Ma'ani; Abu al-Futuh Razi; and Al-Mizan under the verses discussed. This hadith is also found in Manaqib Ibn al-Maghazili al-Shafi'i, p. 265 (Islamiyyah edition).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (١٣)
When the Trumpet is blown with a single blast (13)
وَحُمِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ فَدُكَّتَا دَكَّةً وَٰحِدَةً (١٤)
and the earth and the mountains are lifted and levelled with a single levelling, (14)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١٥)
then, on that day, will the Imminent [Hour] befall (15)
وَٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَهِىَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ (١٦)
and the sky will be split open—for it will be frail on that day— (16)
وَٱلْمَلَكُ عَلَىٰٓ أَرْجَآئِهَا ۚ وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَـٰنِيَةٌ (١٧)
with the angels all over it, and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels]. (17)

Commentary

The Day That Great Event Occurs

Following the opening verses of this Surah, which focused on the issue of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, the verses under discussion present aspects of the events of this Great Resurrection using shaking and awakening expressions that familiarize man with the magnitude of the events ahead.

First, He says: "Then when the Trumpet is blown with a single blast..." (فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ واحِدَةٌ).

As we have previously pointed out, it is understood from the Holy Quran that the end of this world and the beginning of the next will occur with a suddenness and a mighty sound, which is referred to as the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (Nafkha-ye Sur).

This is because, both in the past and today, a trumpet is used to gather and alert an army or to send them to their quarters, played with two different melodies. When the trumpet for rest and sleep is sounded, all soldiers go to their quarters, and when the trumpet for gathering and alert is sounded, all soldiers rise and organize their ranks. It is as if Allah wishes to say that the matter of the end of this world and the beginning of the next is as simple before His power as blowing into a trumpet; with one command and in a sudden moment, all inhabitants of the heavens and the earth die, and with another command, they all don the garment of life and prepare for the Account.

Regarding the specifics of the "Trumpet" (Sur), how it is "blown" into, the number of blasts, and the interval between them, we have provided extensive details in the commentary of Surah Az-Zumar, verse 68 (Volume 19, page 534 onwards), and we see no need to repeat them here.

The only thing that must be mentioned here is that the "Blowing of the Trumpet," as noted above, consists of "two blasts": the "Blast of Death" and the "Blast of Resurrection." As for whether what is mentioned in the verse under discussion is the "first blast" or the "second," commentators do not hold a single opinion. This is because some of the subsequent verses are suitable for the blast of death, while others are suitable for the blast of life and resurrection; however, overall, these verses are more consistent with the first blast, i.e., the blast marking the end of the world.

Then He adds: "And the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed with a single blow" (وَ حُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً). "Dakk," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, originally "means flat and soft ground." Since leveling uneven ground requires crushing it, this word is frequently used to mean a severe crushing.

However, it is understood from some linguistic sources that the primary meaning of "Dakk" is crushing and destroying, and since the consequence of crushing and destroying is becoming flat and level, the word is also used in this sense [1]. In any case, this word in the verse under discussion means the severe crushing of mountains and uneven lands against one another such that they are instantly disintegrated and leveled!

[1]: Aqrab al-Mawarid, root "D-K-K".

Then He adds: "On that Day, the Great Event will occur, and the Resurrection will be established" (فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ).

Not only will the earth and mountains disintegrate, but "the heaven will split asunder, for on that Day it is frail and unstable" (وَ انْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ). The great celestial bodies will not remain exempt from this horrific and terrifying event; they too will be split, scattered, and disintegrated. Despite their current solidity and strength, they will become so frail that, as the Quran says in verse 37 of Surah Ar-Rahman: "And when the heaven is split asunder and becomes rose-colored like [burning] oil" (فَإِذَا انْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهانِ).

In other words, the current heaven and earth will be destroyed, and upon their ruins, a new world will be established that is superior, higher, and more perfect than the current world.

"And the angels will be at its edges and sides" (وَ الْمَلَكُ عَلى‌ أَرْجائِها). "Arja’" is the plural of "Raja," meaning the sides and edges of something. Although "Malak" (angel) is mentioned here in the singular form, it refers to the genus and the collective.

It is as if on that day, the angels, like officers standing around a field awaiting orders to perform a task, will line up around the heavens awaiting the command of the Truth (Allah).

Then He says: "And eight [angels] will, that Day, bear the Throne of your Lord above them" (وَ يَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ).

Although these bearers of the Throne are not explicitly identified in this verse as being angels or otherwise, the overall context of the verse suggests they are angels. However, it is not clear whether they are eight individual angels or eight small or large groups.

Of course, what appears in Islamic narrations is that the bearers of the Throne are currently four individuals (or four groups), but on the Day of Resurrection, they will be doubled. As we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "They are four today, and when the Day of Resurrection comes, He will reinforce them with four others, so they will be eight." [1]

But what is the "Throne" (Arsh)? And who are these angels? Certainly, the "Throne" does not mean a physical royal seat; rather, as we have previously stated in the commentary on the word Arsh, it means the "entirety of the universe," which is the Throne of Allah's sovereignty, and it is administered by the angels who are the executors of Allah's command.

Interestingly, it is mentioned in a narration that "the eight bearers of Allah's Throne on the Day of Resurrection are four from the first [generations] and four from the last. The four from the first are Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus علیهم السلام, and the four from the last are Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali, Hasan, and Husayn علیهم السلام"! [2]

[1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, Vol. 2, p. 384. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 346.

This expression may refer to their status of intercession for the first and the last—intercession, of course, for those who are worthy of it. In any case, it shows the breadth of the concept of the "Throne."

Certainly, if the eight bearers of the Throne are eight groups, it is possible that groups of angels and a group of Prophets and Awliya (Saints) will be responsible for this great task. Thus, part of the administration of the system on that day will be handled by angels, and part by the Prophets, but all by the command of Allah.

Regarding whom the pronoun in "above them" (فَوْقَهُمْ) refers to—whether to "humans" or "angels"—several possibilities have been suggested. Since the preceding sentence speaks of angels, it appears most likely to refer to them. Thus, the angels will encompass the edges of the world, and above them (above in terms of rank) are the eight angels who are the bearers of Allah's Throne.

The possibility also exists that the bearers of Allah's Throne are individuals superior to and higher than the angels, in which case it aligns with the aforementioned Hadith that counts eight of the Prophets and Awliya as the bearers of Allah's Throne.

Undoubtedly, the events related to the Resurrection, let alone matters concerning the bearers of the Throne on that day, are not things that can be precisely clear to us, the inhabitants of this limited and dark world. What we say is a shadow seen from afar in the light of Divine verses; otherwise, one must go and witness the reality! [1]

[1]: Regarding the meanings of "Arsh" (Throne) from a linguistic and Quranic perspective, we have discussed it repeatedly in Tafsir Nemouneh, including in Vol. 6, p. 204 (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf).

It is also necessary to remind that during the first "Blowing of the Trumpet," everyone in the heavens and the earth dies. Therefore, the discussion of the "bearers of the Throne" pertains to the "second blast" when everyone is resurrected. Although there is no mention of the second blast in the verse, it is clear from the context, and the matters we read in the subsequent verses also pertain to this second blast. [1]

[1]: In reality, the verse has an implied (omitted) part, and the estimation is: "Then it is blown into another time."

Ayahs

يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ (١٨)
That day you will be presented [before your Lord]: none of your secrets will remain hidden. (18)
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ هَآؤُمُ ٱقْرَءُوا۟ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (١٩)
As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will say, ‘Here, take and read my book! (19)
إِنِّى ظَنَنتُ أَنِّى مُلَـٰقٍ حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٠)
Indeed I knew that I will encounter my account [of deeds].’ (20)
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ (٢١)
So he will have a pleasant life, (21)
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ (٢٢)
in an elevated garden, (22)
قُطُوفُهَا دَانِيَةٌ (٢٣)
whose clusters [of fruits] will be within easy reach. (23)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَآ أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَيَّامِ ٱلْخَالِيَةِ (٢٤)
[He will be told]: ‘Enjoy your food and drink, for what you had sent in advance in past days [for your future life].’ (24)

Commentary

O People of the Gathering! Read My Record of Deeds

In the commentary of the preceding verses, it was stated that the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (Nafkh al-Sur) occurs twice. In the first instance, all living beings die, and the system of existence disintegrates. In the second instance, a new world is established, and humans and angels find new life. As we mentioned, the beginning of these verses informs of the first blowing, and their conclusion speaks of the second. Continuing this subject, the verses under discussion state: "On that Day, you shall be presented [before Allah]; not a secret of yours shall remain hidden" (يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لا تَخْفى‌ مِنْكُمْ خافِيَةٌ).

"Tu’radun" (تُعْرَضُونَ) is derived from the root "Ardh" (عرض), meaning to show or present something, whether it be goods during a transaction or otherwise. Of course, humans and non-humans are always in the presence of Allah in this world as well, but this reality becomes more manifest and apparent on the Day of Resurrection. For example, Allah’s sovereignty over the universe is eternal, but this sovereignty is more evident on that Day than at any other time.

The phrase "not a secret of yours shall remain hidden" (تَخْفى‌ مِنْكُمْ خافِيَةٌ) may refer to the fact that on that Day, the unseen and the secrets of individuals will turn into the seen and the manifest. As the Quran says regarding the Resurrection: "The Day when secrets are put to the test [and revealed]" (يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ) (At-Tariq: 9).

Not only the hidden actions of humans but also their traits, spiritual states, ethics, and intentions will all be exposed. This is a momentous event, and according to some commentators, it is greater than the disintegration of mountains and the splitting of celestial spheres! It is the day of great disgrace for the wicked and unparalleled honor for the believers—a day when man appears on that stage naked in terms of his deeds and inner secrets. Indeed, on that Day, nothing of our existence remains hidden.

It may also refer to Allah’s encompassing knowledge of everything on that Day, but the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Therefore, it follows by saying: "Then as for him who is given his record in his right hand, he will say [out of extreme joy]: 'Here! Read my record!'" (فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَيَقُولُ هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ‌) ^1. It is as if he is bursting with joy, and for all these blessings, success, and guidance that Allah has granted him, every atom of his being is grateful, and he cries out, "Alhamdulillah" (Praise be to Allah).

Then, summarizing his greatest pride in this sentence, he says:

^1: "Ha’um" (هاؤم), according to linguists, means "Take [it]." If the addressees are a group of men, "Ha’um" is used; if a group of women, "Ha’unna"; if a single male, "Ha’a" (with a fatha); if a single female, "Ha’i" (with a kasra); and for the dual, "Ha’uma." Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Ha’a" is used in the sense of taking, and "Hat" in the sense of giving.

"Indeed, I was certain that I would meet my account" (إِنِّي ظَنَنْتُ أَنِّي مُلاقٍ حِسابِيَهْ‌) ^1. "Dhann" (ظن) in such contexts means "certainty" (Yaqin). He wants to say:

What has become my lot is due to faith in such a day. And truly, it is so; faith in the reckoning and the Book grants man a spirit of piety and God-wariness, creating in him commitment and a sense of responsibility. It is the most important factor in human education.

In the following verses, describing a portion of the rewards for such people, He says: "So he will be in a life of complete satisfaction" (فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ) [^2].

Although He has said everything that needs to be said with this one sentence, for further explanation He adds: "In a lofty Paradise" (فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ). A Paradise so sublime and exalted that no one has seen, heard of, or even imagined the like of it.

[^2]: Although "satisfaction and contentment" is a quality of the possessors of this life, in the above verse, it is made a quality of the life itself, which conveys the ultimate emphasis. That is, it is a type of life that is entirely satisfaction and contentment.

"A Paradise whose fruits are within reach" (قُطُوفُها دانِيَةٌ).

There is no hardship in picking the fruits, nor any difficulty in approaching its fruitful trees. In principle, all its blessings, without exception, are within reach ^1.

And in the final verse under discussion, Allah’s affectionate address to these inhabitants of Paradise is expressed as: "Eat and drink healthfully for what you sent ahead in the days past" (كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِي الْأَيَّامِ الْخالِيَةِ). Yes, these great blessings are not without reason; they are the reward for your deeds which you stored and sent ahead in the world for today. Of course, these meager deeds, when placed in the horizon of Divine grace and mercy, have resulted in such fruits.

Key Points

1- Another Interpretation for the Word "Arsh" (Throne)

1- In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "The bearers of the Throne—and the Throne is Knowledge—are eight: four from us, and four from whomever Allah wills." [^2]

^1: "Qutuf" (قطوف) is the plural of "Qitf" (on the pattern of Hizb), meaning picked fruit, and sometimes it refers to fruit ready to be picked.

[^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 406 (Hadith 28).

In another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is stated: "Those who bear the Throne are the scholars whom Allah has made to carry His knowledge!" ^1 And in another Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read: "The Throne is not Allah; the Throne is a name for Knowledge and Power." [^2] From these Hadiths, it is well understood that the Throne, in addition to the interpretation previously mentioned, has another interpretation: "the Attributes of Allah," attributes such as "Knowledge" and "Power." Therefore, the bearers of the Divine Throne are the bearers of His knowledge, and the more knowledge a human or an angel possesses, the greater their share in bearing this Great Throne!

In this way, the truth becomes clearer that the Throne does not mean a physical chair similar to royal thrones; rather, whenever it is used regarding Allah, it carries various metaphorical meanings.

2- The Status of Ali (عليه السلام) and His Shia

In several narrations, it is stated that the verse "Then as for him who is given his record in his right hand..." was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام or concerning Ali عليه السلام and his Shia [^3]. This is, in fact, an instance of mentioning the clearest examples without limiting the general meaning of the verse.

^1: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 405 (Hadith 26).

[^2]: Ibid, Hadith 27.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 66.

3- Answer to a Question

A question may arise here: Do the believers who, according to the above verses, cry out: "O people of the gathering! Come and read my record of deeds," have no sins at all in their entire record?

The answer to this question can be derived from some Hadiths. Among them, in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that on the Day of Resurrection, Allah first takes a confession from His servants regarding their sins, then He says: "I concealed these sins for you in the world, and today I forgive them." Then (only) the record of his good deeds is given to his right hand ^1. Some have also said that on that Day, Allah transforms the "evil deeds" (Sayyi'at) of the believers into "good deeds" (Hasanat), and it is here that, in the totality of their record of deeds, no point of weakness remains!

^1: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 8, p. 256.

Ayahs

وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِشِمَالِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (٢٥)
But as for him who is given his book in his left hand, he will say, ‘I wish I had not been given my book, (25)
وَلَمْ أَدْرِ مَا حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٦)
nor had I ever known what my account is! (26)
يَـٰلَيْتَهَا كَانَتِ ٱلْقَاضِيَةَ (٢٧)
I wish death had been the end of it all! (27)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنِّى مَالِيَهْ ۜ (٢٨)
My wealth did not avail me. (28)
هَلَكَ عَنِّى سُلْطَـٰنِيَهْ (٢٩)
My authority has left me.’ (29)

Commentary

If Only My Death Had Arrived!

In the preceding verses, the discussion was about the "People of the Right" (أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ) and the believers whose record of deeds is given to their right hand; they call out to the people of the gathering with pride and honor, inviting them to read their deeds, and then they take their place in the eternal Paradise.

However, the verses under discussion address their exact opposite, namely the "People of the Left" (أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ), and through a comparison, they make the situation of both groups perfectly clear. First, He says:

"But as for him who is given his record in his left hand, he will say: 'Oh, I wish that my record had never been given to me!'" (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ فَيَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُوتَ كِتابِيَهْ) [1]

"And I wish I had never known what my account was!" (وَ لَمْ أَدْرِ ما حِسابِيَهْ).

"And I wish my death had been the end (of me) and finished this life of regret!" (يا لَيْتَها كانَتِ الْقاضِيَةَ) [2] Indeed, in that great court, on that "Day of Manifestation" and "Day of Emergence," when he sees all the ugliness of his deeds exposed, his cry rises, and he repeatedly draws burning sighs from his heart—sighs of regret and wails of fire.

[1]: The "ha" in "kitabiyah," as well as in "hisabiyah," "maliyah," and "sultaniyah" which appear in the following verses, is the "ha of silence" (ha al-sakt) or "rest." As we have mentioned, it does not carry a specific meaning but is considered a type of beautiful pause in such words, and it harmonizes with the state and psyche of those uttering these words (reflect on this).

[2]: The phrase "kanat al-qadiyah" has an omitted subject and is estimated as: (kanat hadhihi al-halatu al-qadiyah).

He wishes to completely sever his connection with his past; he wishes for death from Allah, for annihilation, and for salvation from that great disgrace, just as it is mentioned in Surah An-Naba, verse 40: وَ يَقُولُ الْكافِرُ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ تُراباً: "And the disbeliever will say on that Day: 'Oh, I wish I were dust and had never been a human being.'"

Regarding the meaning of the phrase "يا لَيْتَها كانَتِ الْقاضِيَةَ," other interpretations have also been suggested, including: that "qadiyah" refers to the first death, meaning "I wish that when we died in the world, we had never been resurrected." This is while in the world, nothing was more unpleasant to him than death, yet on the Day of Resurrection, he wishes that death had continued! Some have also considered it a reference to the first "Blast of the Trumpet," which is also referred to as "al-Qari'ah," meaning "I wish there were no second blast."

However, the interpretation we mentioned at the beginning is the most appropriate of all.

Then He adds: This sinful criminal begins to confess, saying:

"My wealth has not availed me at all, and it was of no use today, which is the day of my misery" (ما أَغْنى‌ عَنِّي مالِيَهْ).

Not only did my wealth not make me independent or solve any problem for me, "but my power and authority have also perished and vanished from me!" (هَلَكَ عَنِّي سُلْطانِيَهْ). In short, neither wealth was of use, nor was status; and today, empty-handed and in the ultimate humiliation and shame, I am present in the court of Divine Justice. All means of salvation are cut off, my power has vanished into the wind, and my hope is severed from everywhere.

Some have interpreted "sultan" here to mean the proof and argument that leads to a person's victory, meaning: "Today I have no argument or evidence before Allah with which I can justify my deeds."

Some commentators have also said that "sultan" here does not mean authority and governance, because not everyone who enters Hell was a ruler of a country or a prince of a land; rather, it refers to man's authority over his own self and his own life. However, considering that many of the inhabitants of Hell held authority and influence in the world or were among the leaders, this does not seem correct.

Key Points Several Instructive Stories

Many accounts have been narrated here, all of which emphasize the content of the above verses and serve as a lesson for those who rely on wealth and status and are entirely immersed in pride, negligence, and sin, including:

1- In Safinat al-Bihar, it is narrated from the book Nasa'ih: "When Harun al-Rashid's illness became severe in Khorasan, he ordered a physician from Tus to be brought. He then instructed that his urine be presented to the physician along with the urine of a group of other patients and healthy individuals. The physician examined the flasks one after another until he reached Harun's flask. Without knowing whose it was, he said:

'Tell the owner of this flask to make his will, for his strength has dissolved and his constitution has collapsed!'" Upon hearing this, Harun became despondent of life and began reciting these verses: "The physician with his medicine and his drugs—cannot repel a death that has arrived." "Why does the physician die of the same disease—that he used to cure in the past?"

At that moment, he was informed that people were spreading rumors of his death. To dispel the rumor, he ordered a beast of burden to be brought and said, "Mount me upon it." Suddenly, the animal's knees buckled. He said, "Set me down, for the rumor-mongers are right!" Then he ordered shrouds to be brought for him; he liked and chose one from among them and said, "Prepare a grave for me right next to my bed." Then he looked into the grave and recited these verses:

ما أَغْنى‌ عَنِّي مالِيَهْ- هَلَكَ عَنِّي سُلْطانِيَهْ (My wealth has not availed me; my authority has perished from me). And he passed away on that very day. [1]

2- Also in the same book, it is narrated from the honorable scholar Sheikh Baha'i:

"There was a man named 'Tawbah' who frequently engaged in 'self-accounting' (muhasabat al-nafs). One day, he calculated his past life when he was sixty years old. He calculated the total number of days, and it came to 21,500 days. He said: 'Woe to me! If I have committed no more than one sin for every day, I have committed more than twenty-one thousand sins. Do I wish to meet Allah with twenty-one thousand sins?!' At that moment, he let out a cry, fell to the ground, and surrendered his soul to the Creator." [2]

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 523, under the entry "Rushd."

[2]: Ibid, p. 488, under the entry "Dhanb" (adapted).

3- In the book Yatimah by Tha'alibi, it is stated: When the death of Adud al-Dawla approached, his tongue did not move except to recite this verse: ما أَغْنى‌ عَنِّي مالِيَهْ هَلَكَ عَنِّي سُلْطانِيَهْ.

Ayahs

خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ (٣٠)
[The angels will be told:] ‘Seize him, and fetter him! (30)
ثُمَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ (٣١)
Then put him into hell. (31)
ثُمَّ فِى سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَٱسْلُكُوهُ (٣٢)
Then bind him in a chain, seventy cubits in length. (32)
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٣٣)
Indeed he had no faith in Allah, the All-supreme, (33)
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (٣٤)
and he did not urge the feeding of the needy, (34)
فَلَيْسَ لَهُ ٱلْيَوْمَ هَـٰهُنَا حَمِيمٌ (٣٥)
so he has no friend here today, (35)
وَلَا طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ (٣٦)
nor any food except pus, (36)
لَّا يَأْكُلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْخَـٰطِـُٔونَ (٣٧)
which no one shall eat except the iniquitous.’ (37)

Commentary

Take Him and Fetter Him!

In continuation of the previous verses which spoke of the "Companions of the Left"—those who are given their record of deeds in their left hand, causing them to wail, lament, and wish for death—the verses under discussion point to a portion of their punishments in the Hereafter, stating:

"At this moment, the angels of punishment are commanded: Take him and fetter him!" (خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ).

"Ghalloohu" is derived from the root "ghull," which, as previously mentioned, refers to a chain used to bind the hands and feet of criminals to their necks, which was extremely arduous and painful.

"Then it is said: Cast him into the Hellfire" (ثُمَّ الْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ).

"Then thrust him into a chain whose length is seventy cubits" (ثُمَّ فِي سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُها سَبْعُونَ ذِراعاً فَاسْلُكُوهُ). "Silsilah" means a chain, and it is originally derived from the root "tasalsul," meaning movement and vibration, because the links of a chain vibrate and move.

The expression "seventy cubits" may be for the purpose of "multiplication" (takthir), as the number seventy is among those often used to denote a great multitude. It is also possible that the literal number seventy is intended. In any case, such a chain is wrapped around the criminals in a way that it encompasses them from head to toe.

Some commentators have said that these long chains are not for a single individual; rather, each group is bound together by one chain. Mentioning this punishment after the mention of fetters and chains in the preceding verses is more consistent with this meaning.

"Dhira'" (cubit) means "the distance from the elbow to the tip of the fingers" (approximately half a meter), which was the unit of length among the Arabs and is a natural measure. However, some have said that this cubit is different from the common cubit, such that each cubit of it covers vast distances, and all the denizens of Hell are bound by that chain.

Once again, we reiterate that matters related to the Resurrection cannot be fully expressed in the language of us inhabitants of this world, and what appears in the verses and traditions only sketches a shadow of it.

The use of "thumma" (then) in this verse indicates that after entering Hell, he is caught in the seventy-cubit chain, and this is a new punishment for them. There is also the possibility that all these individual and collective chains occur before entering Hell, and "thumma" is used for the sequence of mention.

In the next two verses, the primary cause of this severe punishment is addressed: "For he used not to believe in Allah, the Most Great" (إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ الْعَظِيمِ).

And no matter how much the prophets, saints, and messengers of the Lord invited him toward God, he would not accept. Thus, his connection with the "Creator" was completely severed.

And also, he "did not encourage the feeding of the poor" (وَ لا يَحُضُّ عَلى‌ طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ). In this way, he had also severed his connection with the "creation."

Therefore, the great factor of his misery was the severance of his relationship with the "Creator" and the "creation." From this expression, it is well understood that the bulk of obedience, worship, and Sharia commands can be summarized in these two. The conjunction of "feeding the poor" with "faith" points to the extraordinary importance of this great human act. Indeed, it is so, for as some have said, the worst of beliefs is "disbelief" (kufr), and the worst of moral vices is "stinginess" (bukhl).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 24, p. 470

It is noteworthy that He does not say "he did not feed," but says: he did not urge others to feed. This indicates that, firstly, the feeding by a single individual alone does not solve the problem of the needy; others must also be invited to this good deed so that it takes on a public dimension.

Secondly, a person might not personally have the ability to feed, but everyone has the ability to encourage others.

Thirdly, stingy individuals are such that not only do they not grant or bestow, but they also have no desire for others to grant or bestow.

It is narrated from some of the predecessors that he would instruct his wife to cook more food so that he could give to the poor as well, and then he would say: "We have removed half of that chain from our bodies through faith in Allah, and we remove the other half through feeding [the poor]." [1]

Then He adds: "Since his belief and action were such, he has no devoted friend here today" (فَلَيْسَ لَهُ الْيَوْمَ هاهُنا حَمِيمٌ).

"Nor any food except from the discharge of wounds (ghislin)" (وَ لا طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ). It is noteworthy that their "requital" and "action" are perfectly proportionate. Because of severing the bond with the Creator, they have no warm and intimate friend there; and because of abandoning the feeding of the poor, no food goes down their throats except pus and blood. For they spent years eating the most delicious foods while the destitute had no food but the blood of their own hearts.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Ghislin" means the water that falls from washing the bodies of the disbelievers in Hell. However, it is commonly understood to mean the pus and blood that flows from the bodies of the inhabitants of Hell, and perhaps Raghib's meaning is the same, and the term "food" is also appropriate for this meaning.

A question arises here: in Surah Al-Ghashiyah, verse 6, it says: "No food will there be for them except from a bitter, thorny plant (dari')" (لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ), and "dari'" has been interpreted as a type of "thorn." And in Surah Ad-Dukhan, verses 43-44, we read: "Indeed, the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful" (إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ), and "Zaqqum" has been interpreted as a bitter, foul-smelling, and foul-tasting plant that used to grow in the land of Tihamah and had a bitter and burning sap. How can these verses be reconciled with the verse under discussion?

In response to this question, some have said: All three words ("dari'", "zaqqum", and "ghislin") refer to one thing (which is the harsh and disagreeable plant that is the food of the people of Hell).

Others have said: The inhabitants of Hell are in different levels, and each of these three things is the food for a group of them.

Others have said: Their food is "zaqqum and dari'", and their drink is "ghislin," and the use of the word "food" (ta'am) for a drink is not unprecedented.

In the last verse under discussion, He adds for emphasis: "None eats it except the sinners" (لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ). Some commentators have said: "Khati'" is said of one who intentionally goes after a wrong deed, whereas "mukhti'" is used for both intentional and unintentional acts. Therefore, this hellish food is specifically for those who, with intent and awareness and as an act of rebellion and transgression, pursue the path of polytheism, disbelief, and stinginess.

Key Point: The Beginning of Vowel Marking in Quranic Letters

In a tradition, it is mentioned that Sa'sa'ah ibn Suhan, who was among the companions of Ali عليه السلام, said: An Arab man came to Ali عليه السلام and asked about the verse "لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ". Instead of "khati'un" (sinners), he recited "khatu'un," which means "those who take steps." Therefore, he asked: "All people take steps; does Allah give this food to all of them?"

The Imam smiled and said: "O Arab man, the correct version is لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ." He replied: "You have spoken the truth, O Commander of the Faithful! Allah does not surrender a non-sinning servant to punishment." Then the Imam عليه السلام turned to Abu al-Aswad (who was a man of letters) and said: "Today, even foreigners to the Arabic language have become Muslim; devise a system so they can correct their tongue." Following this command, Abu al-Aswad invented the signs for raf', nasb, and kasr (the vowel marks). [1]

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 51. [1]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur by Jalaluddin al-Suyuti, Vol. 6, p. 263.

Ayahs

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٨)
I swear by what you see (38)
وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٩)
and what you do not see: (39)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (٤٠)
it is indeed the speech of a noble apostle (40)
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ (٤١)
and it is not the speech of a poet. Little is the faith that you have! (41)
وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٢)
Nor is it the speech of a soothsayer. Little is the admonition that you take! (42)
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٣)
Gradually sent down from the Lord of all the worlds. (43)

Commentary

This Quran is Definitely the Word of Allah

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the Resurrection and the fate of the believers and the disbelievers, these verses present an eloquent discourse concerning the Holy Quran and Prophethood, so that the topics of "Prophethood" and "the Hereafter" may complement one another.

First, He says: "I swear by what you see" (فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ).

"And what you do not see" (وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ). It is well-known that the word "la" (no/not) in such instances is extra (za'idah) and used for emphasis. However, some have suggested that "la" here retains its negative meaning, implying: "I do not swear by these matters," because, firstly, there is no need for such an oath, and secondly, an oath must be by the name of Allah. But this view is weak; the first meaning is more appropriate, and in the Holy Quran, oaths by the name of Allah and by things other than His name are frequent.

The phrase "بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ" (by what you see and what you do not see) has a vast meaning that encompasses everything humans see and do not see. In other words, it includes the entire realms of "the manifest" (shahadah) and "the unseen" (ghayb).

Other possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of these two verses, including: that "what you see" refers to the world of creation and "what you do not see" refers to the Creator; or that they refer to manifest and hidden blessings, or humans and angels, or bodies and souls, or this world and the Hereafter.

However, as we noted, the breadth of the concept of these two expressions prevents limiting their meaning. Therefore, everything that falls within the horizon of vision and everything that does not is included in this oath. Yet, its inclusion of Allah seems unlikely, as placing the Creator alongside the creation is not fitting, especially with the use of the expression "ma" (what), which is usually used for non-rational beings.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this expression that what man does not see with his eyes is vast. Modern science has proven this reality—that the senses encompass only a limited circle of existences, and what does not fall within the horizon of the senses, whether in terms of colors, sounds, tastes, waves, or otherwise, is far greater.

The stars that can be seen with the naked eye in both hemispheres of the earth are calculated by scientists to be around five thousand, while the stars that cannot be seen with the eye exceed billions.

The sound waves that the human ear is capable of perceiving are limited, while thousands of other sound waves exist that the human ear cannot hear.

The colors we can see are the seven well-known colors, but today it is certain that countless other colors exist—ultraviolet and infrared—which our eyes lack the power to observe.

The number of tiny organisms that cannot be seen with the eye is so great that they fill the entire world, and sometimes thousands upon thousands of them exist in a single drop of water. Given this, how painful it is that we imprison ourselves in the dungeon of the senses and remain ignorant of, or deny, what lies beyond the senses?

The realm of souls is a world proven by rational and even empirical evidence; it is a world far more expansive than our physical world. Given this, how can one remain confined within the framework of the perceptible?

Then, in the following verse, mentioning the result and the response to this great and unique oath, He says: "Indeed, this [Quran] is the word of a noble Messenger" (إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ). The "Messenger" here undoubtedly refers to the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and not Gabriel, as the subsequent verses clearly testify to this meaning. The reason He says "this is the word of a noble Messenger," while we know the Quran is the word of Allah, is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is the one who conveys it—especially since the title of his "Messengership" (Risalah) is emphasized. We know that what a messenger brings is the word of the one who sent him, even though it is uttered by the messenger's tongue and heard from his blessed lips.

Then He adds: "And it is not the word of a poet; little is it that you believe" (وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ). [1]

"Nor is it the word of a soothsayer; little is it that you reflect" (وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ). In reality, these two verses negate the false attributions that the polytheists and opponents used to ascribe to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Sometimes they said he was a poet and these verses were his poetry, and sometimes they said he was a soothsayer (kahin) and these were soothsaying. This was because soothsayers were people

[1]: "Qalilan" (little) in this verse and the next is an adjective for a deleted "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq), and "ma" is extra (za'idah); the underlying structure is: (tu'minuna imanan qalilan).

who, through connection with jinn and devils, would sometimes disclose some secrets of the unseen, and they particularly accompanied their words with rhymed prose (saj') and rhythmic phrasing. Since the Quran contains both news of the unseen and a unique rhythm, they cast this accusation at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, whereas the difference between the two is as vast as the distance between the earth and the sky.

Some have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation of these verses that the one who attributed poetry and being a poet to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was "Abu Jahl," and the one who attributed soothsaying was "Uqbah" or "Utbah," and others followed them in repeating these slanders.

It is true that the words of the Quran have a beautiful harmony and rhythm that soothes the ear and brings peace to the soul, but this has no connection to the poetry of poets nor the rhymed prose of soothsayers.

"Poetry" is usually the product of imagination and expresses inflamed feelings and emotional excitement; for this reason, it has many ups and downs and fluctuations. In contrast, the Quran, while being beautiful and attractive, is entirely reasoned, logical, and possesses rational content. If it occasionally makes predictions about the future, these predictions do not form the foundation of the Quran; moreover, unlike the reports of soothsayers, they have all been consistent with reality.

The phrases "قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ" (little is it that you believe) and "قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ" (little is it that you reflect) are a rebuke and reproach for those who see this heavenly revelation with its clear signs but sometimes call it poetry and sometimes soothsaying, and seldom believe.

In the final verse under discussion, as an explicit emphasis, He says: "It is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds" (تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ). [1] Therefore, the Quran is neither poetry nor soothsaying, neither the product of the Prophet of Islam's صلى الله عليه وآله thought nor the words of Gabriel; rather, it is the word of Allah which was sent down by the messenger of revelation upon the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This same expression, with slight variations, appears in eleven places in the Holy Quran.

[1]: "Tanzil" is a verbal noun (masdar) used in the sense of a "passive participle" (ism maf'ul) and is the predicate (khabar) of a deleted subject (mubtada); the underlying structure is: "huwa munazzalun min rabbi-l-'alamin" (It is [that which is] sent down from the Lord of the worlds).

Ayahs

وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ (٤٤)
Had he faked any sayings in Our name, (44)
لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٤٥)
We would have surely seized him by the right hand (45)
ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ ٱلْوَتِينَ (٤٦)
and then cut off his aorta, (46)
فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَـٰجِزِينَ (٤٧)
and none of you could have held Us off from him. (47)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٨)
Indeed it is a reminder for the Godwary. (48)
وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنكُم مُّكَذِّبِينَ (٤٩)
Indeed We know that there are some among you who deny [it]. (49)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٠)
And indeed it will be a [matter of] regret for the faithless. (50)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٥١)
It is indeed certain truth. (51)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٥٢)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (52)

Commentary

If He Had Fabricated Lies Against Us, We Would Not Have Given Him Respite!

In continuation of the discussions regarding the Quran, these verses mention a clear proof of its authenticity, stating: "And if he had fabricated against Us some [false] sayings..." (وَ لَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنا بَعْضَ الْأَقاوِيلِ‌).

"We would have seized him by the right hand [with power]" (لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ‌).

"Then We would have cut from him the aorta [the heart-vein]!" (ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ‌).

"And not one of you could have prevented [Us] from him" (فَما مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حاجِزِينَ‌) [^1]. "Aqawil" (اقاويل) is the plural of "aqwal" (اقوال), and "aqwal" is itself the plural of "qawl" (قول).

[^1]: The "min" (مِنْ) in "min ahadin" (مِنْ أَحَدٍ) is extra (za'idah) and serves for emphasis.

Therefore, "aqawil" is a plural form, and what is meant by it here is false sayings.

"Taqawwul" (تقول) comes from the root "taqawwul" (on the weight of takalluf), meaning words that a person invents from himself which have no reality.

The phrase "We would have seized him by the right hand" (لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ‌) means We would have seized and punished him, because the actions a person performs with the right hand possess greater power; thus, "yamin" (right hand) is a metaphor for power [^1]. Some commentators have suggested other possibilities in interpreting this verse, but since they were unfamiliar and discordant, we have disregarded them.

"Watin" (وتين) means the "heart-vein," which is the aorta that delivers blood to all organs. If it is severed, a person's death occurs immediately and without a moment's delay, and this is the swiftest punishment that can be inflicted upon someone.

Some have also interpreted it as the vein by which the heart is suspended, or the vein that carries blood to the liver, or the spinal cord located in the middle of the vertebral column, but the first interpretation seems the most correct of all.

"Hajizin" (حاجزين) is the plural of "hajiz," meaning a barrier or preventer. Here a question arises: if everyone who fabricates a lie against God is immediately punished and destroyed by Him, then all false claimants of prophethood should be quickly annihilated, whereas this is not the case, and many of them lived for long years, and even their false religions remained after them.

[^1]: The "min" (مِنْ) in the above phrase is extra and for emphasis, and the underlying structure is "la-akhadhnahu bil-yamin" (لأخذناه باليمين).

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the Quran does not say "every claimant," but rather says if the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) were to do such a thing—that is, a Prophet to whom God has given miracles and confirmed the proofs of his truthfulness—if he were to deviate from the path of truth, he would not be given a moment's respite, because he would be the cause of the people's misguidance and straying [^1]. However, someone who makes a false claim and has no miracle or clear proof of his truthfulness, it is not necessary for God to destroy him immediately, because the falsehood of his words is clear to anyone seeking the truth. The problem arises when a claimant of prophethood is accompanied by proofs and miracles and then deviates from the path of truth.

From this, it becomes clear that the fact that some "misguided sects" have clung to this verse to prove the claims of their leaders is completely erroneous; otherwise, Musaylimah the Liar and every other false claimant could also argue for their truthfulness using this verse.

In the next verse, again for emphasis and reminder, He says: "And indeed, this Quran is a reminder for the righteous" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌). For those who are ready to purify themselves from sin and follow the path of truth; for those who are seekers and searchers of reality. Those who do not possess this level of Taqwa (piety) certainly cannot benefit from the teachings of the Quran.

[^1]: This is the same concept discussed and deemed repugnant in the books of Theology (Kalam) as "placing a miracle in the hands of a liar."

The deep and extraordinary influence that the Quran has in this regard is itself another sign of its truthfulness.

Then He says: "And indeed, We know that among you are deniers" (وَ إِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنْكُمْ مُكَذِّبِينَ‌). But the existence of a stubborn denier will never be a proof against its truthfulness, for the righteous and the seekers of truth are reminded by it, see the signs of truth in it, and step onto the path of God. Therefore, just as to benefit from the light of a brilliant lamp it is at least necessary for a person to open his eyes, to benefit from the light of the Quran, one must open the eye of the heart to it.

And in the next verse He adds: "And indeed, it will be a cause of regret for the disbelievers" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ‌). Today they deny it, but tomorrow—which is the "Day of Manifestation," the "Day of Emergence," and at the same time the "Day of Regret"—they will realize what a great blessing they lost due to their stubbornness and enmity, and what painful punishments they bought for themselves. On that day, when they see the ranks of the believers and their own depths [of hell] and compare them, they will bite their fingers in regret and gnaw their hands out of intense rage: "And the Day the wrongdoer will bite on his hands and say, 'Oh, I wish I had taken with the Messenger a way'" (Surah Al-Furqan, 27).

And so that no one imagines that the doubt, hesitation, or denial of the rejecters is due to the ambiguity of the Quran's concepts, He adds in the next verse: "And indeed, it is the absolute truth [the truth of certainty]" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَحَقُّ الْيَقِينِ‌).

The expression "Haqq al-Yaqin" (لَحَقُّ الْيَقِينِ‌), according to some commentators, is of the type "adding a thing to itself," because "Haqq" (Truth) is the essence of "Yaqin" (Certainty), and "Yaqin" is the essence of "Haqq," just as we say "Masjid al-Jami'" or "Yawm al-Khamis," and in technical terms, it is an explanatory addition (idafah bayaniyyah).

However, it is better to say it is of the type of adding the "described" to the "attribute," meaning the Quran is a pure certainty. Or in other words, "certainty" has different stages: sometimes it is obtained from rational proof, such as when we observe smoke from a distance and from it obtain certainty of the existence of fire, while we have not seen the fire; this is called "Ilm al-Yaqin" (Knowledge of Certainty).

Sometimes we go closer and see the flames of the fire with our eyes; here the certainty becomes stronger, and it is called "Ayn al-Yaqin" (Vision of Certainty).

Sometimes we go even closer and are placed adjacent to the fire or inside the fire, and we feel its burning with our own hand; certainly, this is a higher stage of certainty which is called "Haqq al-Yaqin" (Truth of Certainty).

The above verse says the Quran is at such a stage of certainty, and yet the blind-hearted deny it!

And finally, in the last verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah Al-Haqqah, He says: "So since it is so, exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great,

And declare Him free from any defect or deficiency" (فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ‌).

It is noteworthy that the content of this verse and the previous verse, with a slight difference, also appears at the end of Surah Al-Waqi'ah, with the difference that here the talk is about the Glorious Quran and its description as "Haqq al-Yaqin," whereas at the end of Surah Al-Waqi'ah, the talk is about the different groups of the righteous and the wicked on the Day of Resurrection.

Key Point

It is noteworthy that in these verses, the "Quran" is described with four attributes: first, it says it is a revelation from the "Lord of the Worlds" (رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌); then it says it is a reminder for the righteous; then it says it is a cause of regret for the disbelievers; and in the final stage it adds: this is "Haqq al-Yaqin." The first is for all people, the second for the pious, the third related to the disbelievers, and the fourth related to the elite and those drawn near [to God].

[Conclusion]

O Allah! You Yourself know that there is nothing superior to the capital of certainty; grant us a faith and certainty that is an instance of "Haqq al-Yaqin."

O Lord! The Day of Resurrection is the Day of Regret; place us at least among those who regret the fewness of their good deeds, not from the commission of many sins and the abandonment of obedience.

O God! Give us our record of deeds in our right hands, and enter us into Your lofty Paradise and Your well-pleasing life.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Haqqah, 5th of Dhu al-Qi'dah, 1406. End of Volume 24 of Tafsir Nemouneh.
